section_header: ## Natural Disasters — ## Efforts for the Future
policy_statement: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.
verbatim_text:
## Efforts for the Future

In response to natural threats, such as floods, inland floods, storm surges, and landslides due to intensified heavy rains and typhoons, TMG will promote the utilization of state-of-the-art technologies and the development of urban facilities in both structural and non-structural aspects.

To cope with increasingly larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains in recent years, TMG will further improve our initiatives.
extraction_rationale: Introduces an umbrella set of measures with multiple structured subcomponents (structural and non-structural) detailed below.
mechanism_description: TMG will promote advanced technologies and develop urban facilities using structural and non-structural measures to better cope with larger typhoons and heavier rains, reducing disaster impacts such as flooding, storm surges, and landslides.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [ecosystem, habitat, tree, trees, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (↑ extreme rain/typhoons) → resilience measures/infrastructure → ↓ exposure & vulnerability → ↓ disaster impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system/activity: Cities plan, build, upgrade, and operate flood/storm resilience infrastructure and implement preparedness/land-use and early-warning measures (structural and non-structural).  
(2) Mediating process: Physical protection and hydrologic/geomorphic risk management (e.g., increased drainage capacity, barriers/levees, retention/storage, slope stabilization) plus improved forecasting/response reduces hazard transmission to people/assets.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced disaster risk and impacts (fewer/inferior floods and storm-surge damages, fewer landslide impacts, lower service disruption and health/safety harms) under current and future climate extremes.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing losses and disruption by lowering exposure and vulnerability to flooding, storm surge, and landslides during extreme rainfall/typhoon events.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: climate hazards (heavier rain, larger typhoons) are anticipated, and measures are implemented to reduce exposure/vulnerability and thus reduce damages—an adaptation risk-reduction pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While some measures can have incidental mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits (e.g., efficient pumps, reduced reconstruction emissions), the principal intended and largest effect is disaster impact reduction, not emissions reduction or energy/material efficiency.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy instance were solely about lowering emissions from disaster response/reconstruction, it could shift toward Mitigation, but that is not inherent to this canonical mechanism; here, the mechanism is explicitly resilience to hazards.  
4) Confirm applicability to ALL instances: Any policy fitting “flood and storm resilience infrastructure and measures → disaster risk reduction” primarily functions by reducing climate risk from extreme events, which is categorically Adaptation.  
5) Secondary subtype note: More granular subtyping (e.g., coastal surge protection vs. pluvial flooding vs. landslide stabilization; structural vs. non-structural) is deferred to policy-level evidence and does not change the primary category.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some projects (e.g., green infrastructure/wetland restoration) may also enhance carbon sinks or resource efficiency, but if the dominant intent/effect remains flood/storm risk reduction, the primary category stays Adaptation; mitigation co-benefits should be treated as secondary at the policy level.
key_indicators: “floods, inland floods, storm surges, and landslides”; “intensified heavy rains and typhoons”; “utilization of state-of-the-art technologies”; “development of urban facilities”; “structural and non-structural aspects”; “cope with increasingly larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains”; “further improve our initiatives”
co_benefits: Potential general disaster-risk reduction and public safety benefits beyond climate-driven events; potential co-benefits for urban service reliability (not specified).
instance_edge_case_notes: Non-structural measures are mentioned but not described (e.g., planning, warnings, zoning), so classification is anchored on the clearly stated “development of urban facilities,” which most strongly signals infrastructure investment rather than a planning/strategy instrument.

section_header: ## (1) Structural measures / ## i Development of rivers, sewerage systems, and coastal conservation facilities
policy_statement: TMG will develop regulating reservoirs (including extending the Wide-Area Regulating Reservoir under Loop Road No. 7 as an underground river), improve river discharge capacity in the Tama area, and implement preventive river maintenance using ICT/AI for inspections.
parent_statement: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.
verbatim_text:
## Measures for rivers against heavy rains

- ' Promote the development of regulating reservoirs under construction, such as the Wide-Area Regulating Reservoir under Loop Road No. 7, in parallel with that of river revetment.
- ' Proceed with the study to operationalize new regulating reservoirs and extend the Wide-Area Regulating Reservoir under Loop Road No. 7 by making it an underground river.
- ' In the Tama area damaged by Typhoon Hagibis in 2019, TMG will improve the discharge capacity of rivers through local improvements.

## Promotion of strategic maintenance of rivers

- ' Steadily implement maintenance based on a preventive maintenance plan...
- ' Ensure more efficient and sophisticated inspections through the introduction of ICT/AI...
extraction_rationale: Specific infrastructure and maintenance actions for rivers with clear verbs ('promote,' 'proceed,' 'will improve,' 'introduce ICT/AI').
mechanism_description: TMG will develop and extend regulating reservoirs, improve river discharge capacity, and use ICT/AI-enabled preventive maintenance to help reduce flooding impacts during heavy rains.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [ecosystem, habitat, tree, trees, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (heavy rainfall/river flooding) → flood management infrastructure & operations → ↓ flood extent/peak discharge → ↓ exposure & damages
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes the built water-management system by adding/expanding regulating reservoirs (detention/underground storage), improving river/channel discharge capacity, and enhancing inspection/maintenance using ICT/AI to keep assets functioning. (2) Physical processes include temporary storage and delayed release of runoff, increased conveyance to reduce overtopping, and reduced failure/blockage risk through better maintenance—together lowering flood peaks and inundation probability/extent. (3) Climate-relevant outcome is reduced vulnerability to intensified precipitation and flood events (less inundation, infrastructure disruption, and economic/social losses).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing flood impacts (exposure/damage) from extreme rainfall by increasing river system capacity and operational reliability.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: addresses a climate hazard (extreme rainfall/river flooding) by increasing system capacity and reliability, leading to reduced flood exposure and damage.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: while there may be minor mitigation co-benefits (e.g., reduced disaster-related emissions) or resource-efficiency aspects, the primary intended and largest effect is risk reduction from climate-driven flooding.  
3) Edge cases applied: if reservoirs were primarily for water supply/hydropower or if measures were mainly to reduce energy use, classification could shift; here, detention/discharge and maintenance are explicitly framed for flood-impact reduction.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy using “river flood management infrastructure → flood risk reduction” has the same dominant hazard-to-risk-reduction pathway, so Adaptation remains the primary category.  
5) Note on subtypes: secondary subtype assignment (e.g., grey infrastructure vs. hybrid/green-grey, early warning integration) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “regulating reservoirs” are operated mainly for water supply/energy and only secondarily for flood control, or if projects include substantial ecosystem restoration (e.g., floodplain reconnection) as the main lever, the primary category could shift (Resource Efficiency or Nature-Based Solutions). Here, the described lever is engineered flood control and operational maintenance, so Adaptation is straightforward.
key_indicators: "Measures for rivers against heavy rains"; "development of regulating reservoirs"; "extend...by making it an underground river"; "improve the discharge capacity of rivers"; "maintenance based on a preventive maintenance plan"; "introduction of ICT/AI"
co_benefits: Reduced routine flood nuisance and disruption; improved public safety and emergency accessibility; potential water-quality/ecosystem co-benefits from better river management (not explicitly stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes both capital works (reservoirs/discharge upgrades) and operational/maintenance modernization (ICT/AI inspections); overall best classified as infrastructure investment because the dominant measures are physical flood-control upgrades.
parent_policy_name: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.

section_header: ## (1) Structural measures / ## i Development of rivers, sewerage systems, and coastal conservation facilities
policy_statement: TMG will develop sewerage trunk lines and storage to handle 75 mm/hour rainfall in high-risk areas, verify sewerage capacity via runoff simulation for 75 mm/hour rainfall, and strengthen countermeasures in newly identified high-risk districts.
parent_statement: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.
verbatim_text:
## Development of sewerage facilities

- ' Develop large trunk lines and storage facilities that can handle precipitation of 75 mm/hour...
- ' By utilizing runoff analysis simulation technology, TMG will verify the capacity of sewerage facilities in case of precipitation of 75 mm/hour, add new districts at high risk of inundation, and strengthen the relevant countermeasures.
extraction_rationale: Contains a quantifiable design standard (75 mm/hour) and concrete infrastructure and analytical actions.
mechanism_description: TMG will develop and verify sewerage trunk lines and storage capacity for heavy rainfall and strengthen countermeasures in high-risk districts to reduce inundation and flood impacts.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (extreme rainfall) → ↑ stormwater/sewerage conveyance + storage → ↓ surface inundation/urban flooding → ↓ exposure and damage
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changed system: Municipal stormwater/sewerage infrastructure is expanded/upsized (trunk lines, detention/storage, and targeted countermeasures in high-risk districts) and planning/verification is improved via runoff simulation. (2) Mediating process: Greater hydraulic capacity and temporary storage reduces surcharge/overflow and peak runoff on streets, lowering flood depth/extent and frequency for a given rainfall intensity. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced vulnerability to heavier downpours (a climate-amplified hazard), resulting in fewer flood impacts on people, property, and critical services.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is lowering urban flood/inundation risk and damage during extreme rainfall events by increasing drainage and storage capacity.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: managing a climate hazard (extreme rainfall) by increasing drainage/storage capacity to reduce inundation impacts.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while construction can have minor mitigation co-effects (embodied emissions) or operational energy changes (pumping), the intended and largest climate-relevant benefit is risk reduction from flooding—an adaptation outcome.  
3) Edge cases applied: if upgrades are primarily for combined sewer overflow water-quality compliance or energy optimization, categorization could shift, but this mechanism is explicitly framed around heavy rainfall and flood/inundation reduction.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: any policy that upgrades stormwater/sewerage capacity to handle heavier rainfall shares the same primary outcome of reduced flood risk/exposure.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., “grey infrastructure flood protection,” “critical infrastructure resilience”) is deferred to policy-level evidence and is not set here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If implemented mainly to reduce wastewater overflows/pollution (water-quality compliance) rather than flood-risk reduction, or if coupled with significant energy-intensive pumping changes, secondary effects could become more salient; however, for this canonical mechanism the primary remains flood-risk adaptation.
key_indicators: “Develop large trunk lines and storage facilities”; “handle precipitation of 75 mm/hour”; “runoff analysis simulation technology”; “verify the capacity of sewerage facilities”; “high risk of inundation”; “strengthen the relevant countermeasures”
co_benefits: Potential water quality/sanitation improvements and reduced combined sewer overflow incidents, but not explicitly stated.
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes an enabling component (capacity verification via runoff simulation and adding new high-risk districts), but the dominant instrument is physical sewerage infrastructure development, so classified as infrastructure-investment rather than planning-strategy.
parent_policy_name: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.

section_header: ## (1) Structural measures / ## i Development of rivers, sewerage systems, and coastal conservation facilities
policy_statement: TMG will set non-binding rainwater runoff control targets (equivalent to 10 mm/hour rainfall) for municipalities, post progress information, consider expanding subsidized prioritized basins, conduct model projects with municipalities, and promote greening to reduce flood damage.
parent_statement: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.
verbatim_text:
## Promotion of measures for river basins

- ' To encourage municipalities to make voluntary and systematic efforts to control rainwater runoff equivalent to precipitation of 10 mm/hour, TMG will set non-binding targets for the amount to be controlled and post information on progress made towards achieving such targets.
- ' Consider adding prioritized basins that will be subsidized...
- ' Carry out model projects in cooperation with municipalities...
- ' Promote efforts to create and conserve greenery and reduce flood damage through a rainwater drainage function.
extraction_rationale: Specific interventions include target-setting (10 mm/hour), subsidies, model projects, and greening for drainage.
mechanism_description: TMG sets non-binding rainwater runoff control targets, shares progress information, supports projects and greening, and these actions increase runoff capture/drainage to reduce flood damage.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (heavy rainfall) → stormwater runoff management → ↓ flood extent/peak flow → ↓ exposure & vulnerability → ↓ climate impact (damage/disruption)
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Municipalities and the metro government set runoff-control targets, coordinate and disclose progress, fund/prioritize basins, implement demonstration projects, and expand green/gray stormwater measures (e.g., detention, retention, infiltration, green infrastructure, drainage upgrades).  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Increased on-site capture and delayed release, higher infiltration/soil storage, added detention/retention volume, and/or improved conveyance reduces runoff peaks and total runoff reaching waterways and low-lying areas during storms.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower probability/severity of pluvial/riverine flooding and associated damages, improving resilience to intensifying precipitation extremes.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced flood damage and disruption from extreme rainfall by lowering runoff volumes/peak flows reaching vulnerable areas.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The policy acts on stormwater systems to reduce flood impacts from heavy rainfall (hazard → reduced exposure/vulnerability → reduced damage).  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: The principal benefit is risk reduction (avoided flood losses), not primarily emissions reduction (Mitigation) or “same service with less energy/material input” (Resource Efficiency).  
3) Edge cases applied: Some stormwater measures can have co-benefits (urban cooling/trees, carbon sequestration, or energy/emissions changes from pumping and construction), but these do not dominate the mechanism’s climate relevance relative to flood-risk reduction.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy under “stormwater_runoff_control → flood_risk_reduction” is inherently aimed at managing runoff to lower flood impacts, which is an adaptation outcome regardless of whether measures are green, gray, voluntary, or mandatory.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any further subtype/co-benefit tagging (e.g., nature-based components, mitigation co-benefits) must be determined at the individual policy level with additional evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If runoff control is pursued primarily for water quality compliance or to increase water supply (rainwater harvesting) with flood reduction incidental, the dominant pathway could shift; however, for this canonical mechanism (explicit flood-risk reduction), Adaptation remains primary. Co-benefits from greening (Nature-Based Solutions) may be substantial but are secondary unless carbon sink enhancement is the dominant stated/expected climate effect.
key_indicators: “set non-binding targets”; “control rainwater runoff equivalent to precipitation of 10 mm/hour”; “post information on progress”; “prioritized basins that will be subsidized”; “model projects in cooperation with municipalities”; “create and conserve greenery”; “reduce flood damage”; “rainwater drainage function”
co_benefits: Urban heat reduction and air-quality/biodiversity improvements from greening; amenity/public health benefits; potential water-quality improvements via runoff reduction.
instance_edge_case_notes: Multiple instruments are bundled (targets + information disclosure + subsidies + demonstration projects). Classified as target-setting because the central, explicitly stated mechanism is establishing non-binding runoff-control targets with progress tracking; funding/model projects are supportive elements.
parent_policy_name: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.

section_header: ## (1) Structural measures / ## i Development of rivers, sewerage systems, and coastal conservation facilities
policy_statement: TMG will use digital technology to improve disaster preparedness functions of agricultural canal reservoirs and intake gates.
parent_statement: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.
verbatim_text:
## Enhancement of agricultural infrastructure

- ' Utilize digital technology to improve the disaster preparedness function of reservoirs and intake gates of agricultural canals.
extraction_rationale: Concrete technology-enabled adaptation action for agricultural water infrastructure.
mechanism_description: Using digital technology improves the disaster preparedness of agricultural canal reservoirs and intake gates, strengthening resilience of water infrastructure during extreme events.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (extreme events) → digital monitoring/control & preparedness → ↓ infrastructure failure/↓ service disruption → ↓ exposure/vulnerability
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human activity/system changed: Water-system operators deploy digital technology (sensors/SCADA, remote controls, forecasting/decision support) to manage agricultural canal reservoirs and intake gates with disaster-preparedness functions.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Improved situational awareness and faster, more optimal gate/reservoir operations (e.g., pre-release, controlled diversion, shutdown/isolation) reduces overtopping, damage, and operational loss during floods or other extremes.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Increased resilience and continuity of water conveyance/supply during climate-driven extreme events, reducing disruption impacts on communities/agriculture and enabling faster recovery.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced exposure and sensitivity of water infrastructure to climate hazards, lowering the risk of failure and water-supply disruption during extreme events.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: climate hazard management via improved preparedness/operations → reduced vulnerability and service disruption (adaptation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While digital upgrades can incidentally improve pumping efficiency (resource efficiency) or reduce emergency energy use (mitigation), the stated purpose and principal effect is resilience to extreme events and continuity of service.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “digital water upgrade” policy were primarily for leak reduction/energy savings, it could shift toward Resource Efficiency/Mitigation; however, this canonical mechanism is explicitly “for disaster preparedness,” anchoring it in Adaptation.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy using digital technology specifically to improve disaster preparedness of reservoirs/gates is principally aimed at reducing climate-risk impacts on water infrastructure, consistent with Adaptation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any additional mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits (e.g., reduced pumping, fewer trucked emergency supplies) should be assessed at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential co-benefits (e.g., improved operational efficiency reducing energy use) may exist, but they are secondary unless the policy’s dominant intent/outcome is energy/GHG reduction rather than hazard resilience.
key_indicators: "Utilize digital technology"; "improve the disaster preparedness function"; "reservoirs and intake gates"; "agricultural canals"
co_benefits: Improved routine water-management efficiency and allocation; reduced labor/operational costs; better asset maintenance/monitoring.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a planning/strategy or performance upgrade if accompanied by operational standards or protocols, but the text reads primarily as implementation of a technology upgrade to infrastructure.
parent_policy_name: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.

section_header: ## (1) Structural measures / ## i Development of rivers, sewerage systems, and coastal conservation facilities
policy_statement: TMG will develop storm surge protection facilities for the eastern lowlands, formulate the next Port of Tokyo Coastal Conservation Facilities Development Plan, develop coastal conservation facilities considering climate impacts, and enable remote floodgate operations and monitoring.
parent_statement: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.
verbatim_text:
## Measures for storm surges

- ' Promote the development of storm surge protection facilities to protect the lives and livelihoods of approximately 3 million people living in the eastern lowlands.
- ' Formulate the next Port of Tokyo Coastal Conservation Facilities Development Plan and promote the development of coastal conservation facilities taking into account the impacts of climate change.
- ' Prepare for emergencies by remotely opening and closing floodgates whenever possible and monitoring camera videos and information signals sent from facilities.
extraction_rationale: Specific infrastructure planning and operational measures; includes an affected population figure (~3 million).
mechanism_description: It promotes storm surge and coastal conservation facilities and supports remote floodgate operation and monitoring to reduce storm-surge flooding impacts and improve coastal resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (storm surge/sea-level-driven coastal flooding) → protective infrastructure + improved operations → ↓ flood exposure/inundation → ↓ damages/disruption
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes the coastal water-management system by constructing/upgrading storm-surge barriers, seawalls/coastal conservation facilities, and enabling remote floodgate operation and monitoring. (2) These measures physically block, divert, or control surge-driven water levels and improve response timing/precision during extreme events. (3) The outcome is reduced frequency/severity of coastal flooding impacts, improving resilience to sea-level rise and intensified storms.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced exposure and damage from storm-surge/coastal flooding through physical protection and improved floodgate operation.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: storm surge/coastal flood hazard is managed via protective infrastructure and operational control to reduce inundation and resulting harm.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: the primary intent and effect is risk reduction/resilience (exposure/vulnerability reduction), not emissions reduction (Mitigation), not primarily reducing resource inputs (Resource Efficiency), and not ecosystem restoration as the main lever (Nature-Based Solutions), even if “coastal conservation” elements exist.  
3) Edge cases applied: coastal defenses can have minor mitigation co-effects (energy use, embodied carbon) and may incorporate natural features, but the dominant effect remains hazard-impact reduction.  
4) Mechanism-wide applicability: any policy fitting this mechanism (storm-surge protection infrastructure + floodgate operations/monitoring) will principally reduce coastal flood risk, so Adaptation applies across instances.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., hard vs hybrid defenses, nature-based components, critical-infrastructure resilience) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a policy under this label is actually implemented primarily as a nature-based coastal buffer (e.g., wetlands/mangroves as the main protective measure), it may shift toward Nature-Based Solutions; likewise, if framed mainly as port/energy efficiency upgrades, classification could differ. For the stated mechanism (storm surge facilities + remote floodgate operation), Adaptation is dominant.
key_indicators: “Promote the development of storm surge protection facilities”; “Coastal Conservation Facilities Development Plan”; “promote the development of coastal conservation facilities taking into account the impacts of climate change”; “remotely opening and closing floodgates”; “monitoring camera videos and information signals”
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: improved port operations reliability and general disaster/emergency management capability; protection of livelihoods/economic activity in lowlands.
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes both a planning element (“Development Plan”) and operational/ICT upgrades (remote floodgate control/monitoring), but the dominant instrument is capital investment in protective infrastructure.
parent_policy_name: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.

section_header: ## (1) Structural measures / ## i Development of rivers, sewerage systems, and coastal conservation facilities
policy_statement: TMG will construct coastal conservation facilities (e.g., detached breakwaters) on islands to protect coastal villages from high tidal waves caused by typhoons and other disasters.
parent_statement: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.
verbatim_text:
## Construction of coastal conservation facilities on the islands

- ' Promote the construction of coastal conservation facilities, such as detached breakwaters, to protect villages etc. along the coast from high tidal waves caused by typhoons and other natural disasters.
extraction_rationale: Concrete coastal infrastructure commitment for island protection.
mechanism_description: Promote the construction of coastal conservation facilities such as detached breakwaters to protect coastal villages from high tidal waves caused by typhoons and other natural disasters.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [ecosystem, habitat, tree, trees, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [ecosystem, habitat, tree, trees, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (storm surge/waves) → coastal protection infrastructure → ↓ hazard energy at shore/↓ inundation → ↓ exposure & damages
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Human activity/system changed: Planning, financing, and constructing coastal defense infrastructure such as detached breakwaters, seawalls, revetments, and related coastal conservation works near vulnerable communities. (2) Mediating physical process: Structures dissipate and reflect wave energy, reduce nearshore wave height, modify currents and sediment dynamics, and (in combination) lower storm-surge-driven overtopping and inundation extent. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced impacts from climate-amplified coastal extremes (e.g., stronger typhoons, higher storm surges on higher sea levels), decreasing damages to housing, infrastructure, and livelihoods and improving safety/resilience.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced coastal flood and wave damage (lower exposure and impacts) from storm surge/typhoons.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism acts by reducing the physical impact of coastal hazards (storm surge/waves) on exposed settlements via protective infrastructure. 2) Why this primary over alternatives: While construction has embodied emissions and may have minor co-benefits (e.g., protecting ecosystems in some designs), the intended and largest climate-relevant effect is risk reduction from climate hazards, which is Adaptation. 3) Edge cases applied: If a project were primarily a “living shoreline”/habitat restoration for protection, it could shift toward Nature-Based Solutions; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly specifies engineered coastal protection infrastructure (e.g., breakwaters), so Adaptation remains dominant. 4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy implementing this mechanism (hard coastal protection to reduce storm surge/wave damage) fundamentally reduces exposure/impacts rather than directly cutting GHG emissions, so Adaptation fits universally. 5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., coastal flooding, critical infrastructure protection) is deferred to policy-level evidence and context.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Hybrid or nature-based coastal defenses (reefs, mangroves, dunes) could warrant Nature-Based Solutions if ecosystem enhancement is the primary means; also, some breakwaters can cause erosion or ecological harm elsewhere (maladaptation risk), but that does not change the primary classification.
key_indicators: "construction of coastal conservation facilities"; "detached breakwaters"; "protect villages etc. along the coast"; "high tidal waves caused by typhoons and other natural disasters"
co_benefits: Potential coastal erosion control, habitat/sediment management, and protection of fisheries/tourism assets (not explicitly stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: “Other natural disasters” broadens beyond climate hazards, but explicit linkage to typhoons/high tidal waves is sufficient to classify as direct climate adaptation infrastructure.
parent_policy_name: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.

section_header: ## ii Protection of functions of urban facilities
policy_statement: TMG will promote removal of utility poles and strengthen support for municipality roads under the Strategy for Accelerating the Removal of Utility Poles.
parent_statement: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.
verbatim_text:
## Promoting the removal of utility poles

- ' Promote the removal of utility poles to prevent their collapse during earthquakes, storms and flooding, and facilitate a smooth response to disasters.
- ' Work to strengthen support for municipality roads in addition to Tokyo metropolitan roads and efforts for urban development based on the Strategy for Accelerating the Removal of Utility Poles.
extraction_rationale: Specific infrastructure resilience action (undergrounding/removal of poles) tied to a named strategy.
mechanism_description: It promotes removing utility poles and strengthening road support to reduce collapse risks during earthquakes, storms, and flooding and to facilitate a smoother disaster response.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (storms/flooding/earthquakes) → hardened/undergrounded corridor infrastructure → ↓ physical damage + ↓ service disruption → ↓ exposure/vulnerability (maintained access and response)
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes the built environment by removing vulnerable roadside utility poles (often via undergrounding/relocation) and strengthening road foundations/embankments/drainage and structural supports. (2) Improves structural integrity and reduces failure modes from wind, inundation, scour, debris impact, and ground shaking; also reduces obstruction of roadways from pole collapse and associated line hazards. (3) Climate-relevant outcome is improved disaster resilience—fewer infrastructure failures, faster emergency response and recovery, and reduced societal vulnerability during extreme events.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced exposure and disruption from extreme events (e.g., flooding/storms) through more resilient road/utility infrastructure.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: hazard event → reduced infrastructure failure/obstruction → reduced disruption and vulnerability (a resilience outcome).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while construction choices can affect emissions (materials, construction activity) and resource use, the intent and main effect across this mechanism is to withstand hazards and maintain function, which is Adaptation.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a specific policy were framed primarily around emissions reductions (e.g., pole removal solely to enable EV charging or grid efficiency) it might shift categories, but that would be a different mechanism; here the stated causal chain is collapse-risk reduction and disaster response. Earthquake resilience is not climate-specific, but the same hardening also addresses climate-driven storms/flooding; the climate-relevant classification remains Adaptation because it reduces vulnerability to extreme events.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: any policy under “utility pole removal and road hardening → disaster resilience improvement” is fundamentally about reducing damage/disruption from hazards, which is adaptation regardless of locale.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., flood vs. storm vs. multi-hazard resilience; transport continuity vs. emergency access) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Includes non-climate hazards (earthquakes), but the dominant climate-relevant effect is still reduced vulnerability to extreme weather and flooding; minor mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits from reduced outage-related losses or avoided emergency detours are secondary.

section_header: ## ii Protection of functions of urban facilities
policy_statement: TMG will build redundancy in roads/bridges for disasters by building a highway network, constructing new bridges (including along borders with Chiba), improving access roads to the Tachikawa Wide-Area Disaster Management Base, and upgrading emergency transport routes (bridges, alternative routes, widening).
parent_statement: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.
verbatim_text:
## Development of roads and bridges

- ' Build a highway network to ensure redundancy 1 in the event of a disaster.
- ' Promote the construction of new bridges along sections that border Chiba Prefecture...
- ' Improve accessibility to the Tachikawa Wide-Area Disaster Management Base...
- ' Improve the disaster preparedness of Tokyo... through the construction or replacement of bridges along emergency transport routes, the development of roads as alternative routes..., and the widening of emergency transport roads.
extraction_rationale: Multiple concrete transport infrastructure commitments to maintain evacuation/logistics during disasters.
mechanism_description: Build and improve highways, bridges, and emergency transport routes to add redundancy and improve access for disaster response, strengthening resilience during disasters.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard → ↑ infrastructure robustness/redundancy → ↓ service disruption & ↓ exposure/vulnerability during events
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Invest in and redesign critical transport assets (highways, bridges, emergency routes, access roads) to be more robust and redundant, and to preserve connectivity to disaster management bases. (2) Mediating process: Improved structural resilience and network redundancy reduces the probability of critical link failure and enables rerouting, faster emergency access, evacuation, and logistics under hazard conditions. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower vulnerability and damages from climate-amplified disasters (e.g., storms, flooding, heat-related degradation) through maintained mobility and response capability.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced disruption and harm during extreme events by maintaining emergency mobility and access for response and evacuation.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: hazard-facing transport resilience measures that reduce disruption and vulnerability during disasters. 2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: while construction may affect emissions and efficiency, the mechanism’s intended and largest climate-relevant effect is coping capacity and reduced impact under extreme events, not emissions reduction (Mitigation) or reduced resource inputs (Resource Efficiency). 3) Edge cases applied: if upgrades were primarily low-carbon transport expansion or congestion reduction, mitigation/resource-efficiency could dominate; here, redundancy, emergency routes, and disaster management access clearly indicate adaptation. 4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy that upgrades emergency transport infrastructure for disaster response resilience primarily functions to reduce vulnerability to hazards, so Adaptation remains the primary category. 5) Note: secondary subtype assignment (e.g., flood-proofing vs seismic vs heat resilience; co-benefit mitigation impacts from smoother traffic) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential co-benefit/impact reversal: added highway capacity can increase vehicle-km traveled and emissions, but this is not the dominant stated mechanism; classification stays Adaptation unless the primary intent/effect is capacity expansion for general mobility rather than disaster resilience.

co_benefits: Improved everyday mobility and logistics efficiency; potential economic development/connectivity benefits; general safety improvements (non-climate disasters)
instance_edge_case_notes: Mentions "disaster" broadly rather than explicit climate hazards; however, the redundancy/robustness investments in critical transport networks are commonly climate-relevant adaptation (e.g., storms/flooding), so climate relevance is treated as direct.

section_header: ## ii Protection of functions of urban facilities
policy_statement: TMG will diagnose and systematically renew roadside trees in areas with many fallen trees to strengthen their disaster preparedness function.
parent_statement: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.
verbatim_text:
## Strengthening the disaster preparedness function of roadside trees

- ' To strengthen the disaster preparedness function of roadside trees, TMG will focus on the diagnosis of roadside trees in areas with many fallen trees due to typhoons for their systematic renewal.
extraction_rationale: Specific intervention (diagnosis and renewal) targeting typhoon-related treefall risk.
mechanism_description: TMG will diagnose roadside trees and carry out systematic renewal to strengthen their disaster preparedness function, with co-benefits from healthier urban tree cover that can reduce heat and store carbon.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (extreme heat) → ↓ urban heat exposure/temperature → ↓ heat morbidity/mortality and heat-related disruption
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Cities expand, protect, and better manage urban tree canopy (street trees, parks, riparian corridors, connected green networks), often prioritizing neighborhoods with low canopy and high heat vulnerability.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological processes: Trees provide shade (reducing shortwave radiation on surfaces/people) and evapotranspiration (latent cooling), lowering local air and surface temperatures and improving thermal comfort; secondarily, biomass growth stores carbon in woody tissue and can increase soil carbon.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Primary outcome is reduced heat risk (lower exposure and health impacts during heatwaves); secondary outcome is modest net CO₂ removal/storage (and sometimes reduced building cooling demand).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies expanding urban tree canopy is reducing urban heat exposure through shading and evapotranspiration (urban heat mitigation).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The most direct and typically largest effect is heat mitigation—trees lower urban temperatures and exposure during extreme heat events.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although carbon sequestration is explicitly mentioned, urban tree sequestration is generally smaller and slower relative to the immediate, localized heat-risk reduction benefits; therefore the dominant pathway aligns with Adaptation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy were primarily designed/credited as a carbon-offset/removal program with rigorous MRV and carbon accounting as the central objective, Mitigation or Nature-Based Solutions could become primary at the policy level; however, for the shared mechanism “urban canopy expansion for heat mitigation + sequestration,” the heat-risk reduction pathway remains the most consistently dominant across instances.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that expands urban tree canopy inherently increases shading/evapotranspiration and thus contributes to heat mitigation, regardless of exact planting location or governance details; hence Adaptation fits all policies sharing this mechanism.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Co-benefits (carbon sequestration, energy-demand reduction, biodiversity, stormwater) and the strength of the sequestration claim depend on species choice, survival rates, maintenance, land tenure, and accounting—these should be assigned based on policy-specific evidence in a later step.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some urban tree programs are framed primarily as “urban carbon sinks” or are embedded in carbon-crediting schemes (which could shift the dominant effect toward Mitigation/Nature-Based Solutions for that specific policy). Conversely, if the policy mainly targets biodiversity/green-network connectivity with minimal canopy gain, the mechanism match weakens. Also note potential countervailing effects (maintenance emissions, irrigation energy/water use, biogenic VOCs affecting ozone) are context-dependent and usually do not overturn the primary heat-adaptation pathway.
key_indicators: “strengthen the disaster preparedness function”; “diagnosis of roadside trees”; “areas with many fallen trees”; “due to typhoons”; “systematic renewal”
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: urban heat reduction/shade, air-quality benefits, amenity/biodiversity, modest carbon storage
instance_edge_case_notes: Primary hazard focus in the text is typhoons (wind/fallen trees) rather than extreme heat; despite the Stage-2 pathway referencing heat, the instrument here is adaptation via storm resilience/safety and continuity of transport/urban services. Also could be interpreted as an operations/maintenance investment, but no explicit budget/capital works are stated, so “planning-strategy” fits best.
parent_policy_name: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.

- ' Bury pipelines that cross rivers, as surface pipes may be affected by river flooding...
extraction_rationale: Concrete water infrastructure resilience measures to prevent service disruption during floods.
mechanism_description: It improves water-supply stability by expanding and making water conveyance and transmission pipelines more redundant and less vulnerable to flooding damage.

primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (flooding) → ↓ infrastructure damage / ↓ service disruption → ↓ exposure & vulnerability of water users
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: The city expands, hardens, and duplicates critical water conveyance and transmission assets (e.g., dual/looped networks, buried/river-crossing protections) to create redundancy and reduce single points of failure. (2) Mediating process: Flood waters and associated scour/impact cause less physical damage and fewer network breaks, and if one line fails the redundant pathway maintains hydraulic connectivity and pressure. (3) Outcome: More reliable water supply during extreme events, reducing climate-related service interruption risk for households and critical facilities.

mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Flood hazard affects conveyance assets; upgrades and redundancy reduce damage and maintain service—this is a classic exposure/vulnerability reduction pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While some projects may incidentally improve pumping efficiency (Resource Efficiency) or affect energy use/GHGs (Mitigation), the mechanism described is explicitly about resilience to flooding and supply stability, making Adaptation the dominant effect.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a conveyance upgrade were justified mainly for leakage reduction/energy savings, it could shift toward Resource Efficiency/Mitigation, but that is not inherent to this canonical mechanism as defined (redundancy + flood vulnerability reduction).  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy implementing “upgrade/expand conveyance/transmission for redundancy and flood robustness” primarily functions to reduce climate-disruption risk to water supply, fitting Adaptation across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any secondary mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits (e.g., reduced leaks, optimized pumping, embedded emissions from construction) require policy-specific evidence and are not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some conveyance upgrades can also reduce leakage and pumping energy (resource efficiency/mitigation co-benefits) or increase water availability (supply augmentation); however, under this mechanism the defining feature is hazard-robustness and redundancy for continuity during floods, so Adaptation remains primary.
key_indicators: "Double water conveyance facilities"; "Construct wide-area water transmission pipe networks"; "promote a dual system"; "Bury pipelines that cross rivers"; "affected by river flooding"; "improve the stability of water supply"
co_benefits: Improved day-to-day reliability/maintenance resilience of the water network; potential reduction in leakage and outage-related economic losses.

(1) Change in system: The city upgrades subway flood defenses (e.g., barriers, waterproofing, drainage/pumping) and implements early-restoration planning to keep tunnels/stations/critical systems functional or quickly recover after inundation.  
(2) Mediating process: Physical floodwater exclusion/management and improved system resilience reduce inundation depth/duration and accelerate repair/restore of power, signaling, and station access.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower flood impacts on transportation—reduced asset damage, reduced downtime, improved continuity of mobility and emergency access during extreme precipitation/flood events.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced exposure and sensitivity of transit infrastructure and riders to flood hazards, resulting in less damage and shorter service disruption during floods.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Flood hazard acting on transit assets is met with protective and rapid-recovery measures that reduce damage and disruption—an exposure/vulnerability reduction pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While improved transit reliability could indirectly affect mode choice and emissions, the direct and intended effect is managing climate hazard impacts, not reducing GHGs or energy use.  
3) Edge cases applied: If flood works were paired with major energy-efficiency upgrades or electrification, those would be separate mitigation mechanisms; here, the mechanism is explicitly flood control/early restoration.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy under this mechanism—flood protection and rapid restoration for transit infrastructure—primarily functions to reduce flood damage/service disruption, which is adaptation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any secondary mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits (e.g., avoiding replacement emissions, avoiding car trips during outages) require policy-specific evidence and are not used for the primary category.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential secondary mitigation co-benefits (avoided reconstruction emissions; avoided temporary shift to cars during transit outages) can occur, but they are not the dominant climate-relevant effect of this mechanism.

co_benefits: Improved transit reliability and safety during non-climate disruptions; reduced general maintenance/repair costs from water damage; enhanced emergency response access via continued subway operations.

section_header: ## iv Landslide measures

1) Changed human activity/system: Land-use planning and hillside management are altered via erosion control works, maintenance of slope protection infrastructure (e.g., anchors/retaining systems), forest management for slope stability, and (where needed) relocation away from high-risk zones.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Improved slope stability and drainage reduces soil saturation and shear failure likelihood; maintained/strengthened protection structures reduce collapse probability; vegetation/forest condition can improve soil cohesion and reduce surface erosion; relocation reduces the number/value of assets in hazard zones.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower landslide hazard and exposure under current and future rainfall regimes, reducing climate-related disaster losses and improving resilience.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced loss of life and damage from landslides by decreasing hazard probability/intensity and/or reducing exposure in landslide-prone areas.

2) Why this primary over alternatives: While forest management may have minor mitigation co-benefits (carbon), and infrastructure upgrades may change material/energy use, the mechanism’s defining intent and largest climate-relevant effect is reducing disaster risk from landslides.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy framed as “forest improvement” were primarily for carbon sequestration, it would shift toward Nature-Based Solutions/Mitigation; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly targets landslide hazard/exposure reduction, keeping Adaptation dominant.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that uses landslide risk reduction measures to reduce landslide hazard/exposure primarily functions to reduce climate-related impacts, regardless of local context or specific measures (engineering, ecosystem management, or relocation).  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further tagging (e.g., “nature-based” vs “grey infrastructure,” “managed retreat,” “early warning”) requires policy-level evidence and is not set at the mechanism level here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Policies combining slope stabilization with large-scale afforestation/carbon projects could have a mixed objective; classify by the dominant effect—if landslide risk reduction is primary, keep Adaptation, otherwise consider Nature-Based Solutions/Mitigation. If measures are mainly routine infrastructure maintenance unrelated to climate hazards, climate relevance may be indirect but the mechanism still maps to Adaptation.
key_indicators: "Prioritize erosion control work"; "landslide prone areas"; "locations where landslides have actually occurred"; "Promote preventive maintenance management"; "slope protection facilities"; "ground anchors"; "Strengthening the disaster preparedness function of forests"; "thinning and pruning"; "Develop disaster-resistant forests"; "encourage relocation to safer areas"; "making regional structure consolidated"
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: improved forest health/biodiversity, reduced sediment runoff/water quality impacts, general disaster-risk reduction for non-climate triggers (e.g., earthquakes), improved public safety and continuity of services.
instance_edge_case_notes: Multiple instrument types are bundled (infrastructure investment + planning/strategy for regional consolidation/relocation + ecosystem/forest management program). Classified by the dominant, most concrete measures emphasized: erosion control and slope protection maintenance (infrastructure/asset management).
parent_policy_name: TMG will promote structural and non-structural measures for natural disasters using state-of-the-art technologies and urban facility development, and further improve initiatives to cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains.

## Strengthening disaster response in rescue activities

- ' Strengthen the flood prevention activity system by developing personal materials and equipment for flood disasters.

mechanism_description: The plan strengthens flood response by developing personal flood-disaster materials and equipment, improving readiness and reducing harm from flooding events.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]

causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in system/activity: City and residents improve flood-disaster readiness by developing/procuring personal flood-response materials and equipment and strengthening response procedures. (2) Mediating process: Faster warning, evacuation, first response, and protective actions reduce hazard contact and shorten disruption duration during inundation. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced human and economic impacts from flooding (injuries/fatalities, asset damage, downtime), increasing resilience to heavier rainfall/sea-level-rise-driven flood risk.

Resource efficiency is not the central lever (it is not primarily about delivering the same service with less energy/material), and nature-based solutions are not involved (no ecosystem enhancement). 3) Edge cases applied: even if equipment includes energy-efficient pumps or low-carbon materials, the core intent and dominant effect remain emergency preparedness and impact reduction. 4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: “flood response capacity building → flood impact reduction” consistently targets preparedness/response to lessen flood damages, fitting adaptation across contexts. 5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., emergency management, disaster risk reduction, public health resilience) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a policy under this mechanism primarily funds physical flood-control infrastructure (levees/drainage upgrades) rather than response capacity, it may warrant a different canonical mechanism; however, for capacity-building via materials/equipment/readiness, Adaptation remains correct.
